Every time a PokÃ©mon Go player pointed their phone at a building, a street corner, a park bench â Nianticâs system was quietly geo-tagging that image and adding it to a growing database. Nobody asked. Nobody noticed. Everyone just wanted the Pikachu.
30 billion photos later, Niantic had built one of the most detailed real-world 3D navigation datasets ever assembled. Street View took Google years and a fleet of specialised camera cars. PokÃ©mon Go did it faster â using 500 million volunteers who had absolutely no idea they were working.
The result? Robots that can now navigate real-world environments within centimetres of accuracy â without GPS. Using visual recognition trained on images your phone quietly contributed while you were chasing a Snorlax in 2016.
This is what makes modern AI so powerful and so unsettling simultaneously.
The training data was never the algorithm. It was always the humans who didnât read the terms and conditions.
